title: Have We Met Before?

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: At a party of 6 people, every pair has either MET each other before, or has NOT met each other before.
rules: Each pair of people is either 'acquainted' (met before) or 'strangers' (never met).
We want to find a TRIPLE -- three people who are PAIRWISE acquainted, OR three people who are PAIRWISE strangers.
setup.question: Prove that among any 6 people at a party, there exist either 3 mutual acquaintances or 3 mutual strangers.

learningObjectives: Fix one person and partition the other 5 by their relationship to that person.
Apply pigeon hole: 5 others into 2 buckets means at least 3 share a bucket.
Case-analyze inside the 3+ bucket to extract a mutual triple.

stages.label: The set-up move
stages.prompt: Pick one person, call her Alice. Among the OTHER 5 people, each is either an ACQUAINTANCE of Alice or a STRANGER. By Pigeon Hole, at least how many fall into one of these two categories?

options.text: At least 3 -- because 5 in 2 buckets forces ceil(5/2) = 3 in one bucket.

stages.explanation: 5 pigeons (people other than Alice), 2 holes (acquaintance/stranger). Generalized pigeon hole: ceil(5/2) = 3, so at least one hole has 3 people.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Pigeon Hole + case analysis: fix Alice, find 3+ in one bucket, then case-analyze the relationships AMONG those 3.
Probability over random graphs of size 6.

stages.explanation: Pigeon Hole gives us the structural constraint (3 share a relationship to Alice). Case analysis on the relationships among those 3 finishes the proof.

stages.label: Case 1: 3 acquaintances of Alice
stages.prompt: Suppose 3 of the others (call them B, C, D) are all acquainted with Alice. Now consider the relationships among B, C, D themselves. What conclusion can we extract?

options.text: If ANY pair among B,C,D are acquainted, then Alice + that pair = 3 mutual acquaintances. Otherwise B,C,D are PAIRWISE strangers = 3 mutual strangers.
B, C, D must be mutual strangers.
We can't conclude anything without more information.

stages.explanation: Two sub-cases: either at least one pair (B-C, B-D, or C-D) is acquainted -- combine with Alice to get a mutual triple of acquaintances. Or NO pair is acquainted -- then B, C, D themselves are 3 mutual strangers.

stages.label: Case 2: 3 strangers of Alice
stages.prompt: Suppose instead 3 of the others (B, C, D) are all STRANGERS to Alice. Now consider relationships among B, C, D. What conclusion?

options.text: If ANY pair among B,C,D are strangers, then Alice + that pair = 3 mutual strangers. Otherwise B,C,D are PAIRWISE acquaintances = 3 mutual acquaintances.
Same as Case 1 -- the conclusion is identical.
B, C, D must be mutual acquaintances.

stages.explanation: Mirror image of Case 1. Either some pair is strangers -- triple with Alice -- or all pairs are acquainted, giving B,C,D as 3 mutual acquaintances.

stages.label: Commit

options.text: Yes -- both cases conclude with either 3 mutual acquaintances or 3 mutual strangers.
No -- we need to handle Alice having exactly 3 of each.

stages.explanation: Both cases (3 acquaintances of Alice / 3 strangers of Alice) lead to the conclusion. Pigeon Hole guarantees at least one case applies. This is the smallest case of Ramsey's theorem: R(3,3) = 6.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Could the conclusion fail with only 5 people instead of 6? Construct or imagine a counterexample.
What is R(3,4)? R(4,4)? (These are the next Ramsey numbers -- they grow surprisingly fast.)
Why is the bound 6 tight? Find a 5-person example where neither 3 mutual acquaintances nor 3 mutual strangers exist.
